coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive quercetin electrochemical sensor based on reduced graphene oxide/β-cyclodextrin/graphene quantum dots/molybdenum trioxide composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Li
Chen, Xiaoling
Li, Jiejun
Li, Yuhong
Chen, Jia
Zhang, Mengting
Shi, Jaqi
Yang, Pingping
Zhao, Pengcheng
Fei, Junjie
Xie, Yixi
coredata.dc:description: Quercetin is one of the flavonoids with antioxidant activity and has attracted great interest. Accurately and rapidly detection of quercetin belongs is of great significance. Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemical quercetin sensor based on reduced graphene oxide/β-cyclodextrin/graphene quantum dots/molybdenum trioxide (rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3) was constructed. The rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3 presented excellent electrochemical detection for quercetin benefit from the excellent conductivity and adsorption enrichment effect of rGO-β-CD, good dispersion and stability of GQDs and electrocatalytic activity of MoO3. Under optimal conditions for the detection of quercetin, the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE showed wider linear range (5–2600 nM) and lower detection limit (LOD, 1.5 nM, (S/N = 3)). Furthermore, the sensor we constructed shows good accuracy and stability for the detection of quercetin in actual sample detections.

MoO3
Electrochemical sensors
Quercetin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
Electrochemical behavior of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE at different scan rates
Quantitative Analysis of Que on rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE
Detection of Que in actual samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 22176160 , 21874114 , and 21475114 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province ( 2021JJ30662 ), the Project of Innovation Team of the Ministry of Education ( IRT_17R90 ), the Project of Science and Technology Innovation Plan of Hunan Province ( 2021WK2006 ), and the Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province ( CX20210632 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) MoO3, (B) GO, (C, D) rGO-β-CD and (E, F) rGO-β-CD/GQDs composites.

(A) EIS spectra of bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, MoO3/GCE, rGO-β-CD/GCE, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE and rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE. Supporting electrolyte: 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4-; Frequency range: 0.1 Hz to 100 KHz. (B) CV of 1 μM Que on bare GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE (c), MoO3/GCE(d), rGO-β-CD/GCE (e) and rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE (f) in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 6.0).

(A) CVs of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE at different pH (from a to h: 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0) in 0.1 M PBS containing 5 μM Que. (B) The dependence of oxidation peak potential (Epa), formal potential (Eθ), reduction peak potential (Epc) on pH.

(A) CVs of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE at different scan rates (from a to t: 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400 mV⋅s-1) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0) containing 5 μM Que. (B) The dependence of redox peak currents on the scan rates (v). (C) The dependence of oxidation peak potential on lnv.

(A) DPVs obtained from rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE in the presence of Que ranging from a to o (5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 2100, 2600 nmol/L) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0); (B) The DPVs of linear calibration between the current responses and the concentration of Que.

(A) The stability of the sensor was studied in 0.1 M PBS butter solution with 11 cycles of tested. (B) Reproducibility study of seven individual electrodes in the same conditions. (C) Effect of some possible interfering substances on the determination of 1 μM Que in PBS solution.
Schematic representation of the fabrication procedure of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE.
Comparison of different modified electrodes for detecting Que.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive quercetin electrochemical sensor based on reduced graphene oxide/β-cyclodextrin/graphene quantum dots/molybdenum trioxide composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmentally Friendly Chemistry and Applications of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmentally Friendly Chemistry and Applications of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Huaihua University, Huaihua 418000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Organic Synthesis and Application of Hunan Province, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Green Organic Synthesis and Application of Hunan Province, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Institute of Advanced Sensing and Information Technology, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondenece to: College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quercetin is one of the flavonoids with antioxidant activity and has attracted great interest. Accurately and rapidly detection of quercetin belongs is of great significance. Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemical quercetin sensor based on reduced graphene oxide/β-cyclodextrin/graphene quantum dots/molybdenum trioxide (rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3) was constructed. The rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3 presented excellent electrochemical detection for quercetin benefit from the excellent conductivity and adsorption enrichment effect of rGO-β-CD, good dispersion and stability of GQDs and electrocatalytic activity of MoO3. Under optimal conditions for the detection of quercetin, the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE showed wider linear range (5–2600 nM) and lower detection limit (LOD, 1.5 nM, (S/N = 3)). Furthermore, the sensor we constructed shows good accuracy and stability for the detection of quercetin in actual sample detections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin (Que), a common antioxidant, is one of the flavonoids frequently consumed in human daily diet. It widely exists in medicinal plants, flowers and fruits [1]. Que has a variety of biological activities, such as anti-oxidation [2], anti-diabetic [3] and anti-asthmatic effects [4]. It has shown good effects in the treatment of prostate cancer and inhibition of the growth of isolated malignant cells [5,6]. However, excessive use of Que may be harmful to human health. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a rapid, highly sensitive and accurate Que detection method. Some traditional methods have presently been used to detect Que, such as chromatography [7], spectrophotometry [8], and chromatography/mass spectrometry [9]. However, due to the complex process, long time-consuming and high-cost, its application in the field of fast and field sensing is limited. Therefore, the electrochemical sensor with simple, fast, low cost and good stability has attracted much attention [10–17].

β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD), with relative low production cost, unique cavity structure and good bioavailability, has obtained much attention [18,19]. In addition, owing to its molecular recognition, inclusion complex and selective, β-CD has attracted much attention [20–24]. For example, Zhou et al. constructed a quercetin electrochemical sensor by using Au-β-CD/NH2-GQDs nanoparticles [25]. Zhao et al., prepared a quercetin electrochemical sensor by using MoS2 -carbon nanotube @ graphene oxide nanoribbons / HS-cyclodextrin /graphene quantum dots composite [13].

Reduced graphene oxide (rGO), obtained by reduction of graphene oxide (GO), has been empolyed in many electrochemcial sensors due to its big specific surface area and high mass transfer efficiency [26]. GQDs has poor conductivity, but it can show its advantages of dispersion, stability and biocompatibility when combined with other carbon materials [27]. According to the special properties of β-CD, GQDs and rGO, the composite of rGO-β-CD/GQDs may propbably combine the advantages of the three single materials to exhibite good conductivity, dispersion and molecular recognition ability.

Molybdenum trioxide (MoO3) has been attracted much attention because of the unique layered structure, anisotropy, high surface area, excellent electron radial transmission and high electrochemical activity, which was widely used for catalysis, supercapacitors, batteries and electrochemical sensing [28–32]. However, with the poor conductivity of MoO3, which limits its application in electrochemical sensors. Interestingly, the addition of conductive materials can solve the drawback. For example, Qin Zeng et al. developed luteolin sensor based on MoO3 polypyrrole nanowires /MWCNTs composite material [33]. MoO3 @GCNS exhibited a very sensitive electrochemical detection of caffeine [34]. Combined with conductive materials, MoO3 showed its unique advantages, thus improving the detection performance of the sensor.

Herein, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3 composites were successfully prepared. And, an ultra-sensitive Que electrochemical sensor was successfully constructed based on the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3 composites. Due to the rGO-β-CD with excellent conductivity and adsorption enrichment effect, GQDs with good dispersion and stability, as well as electrocatalytic activity of MoO3, the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE showed excellent electrochemical response to Que.

β-cyclodextrin and graphite powder were purchased from shanghai macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Manganese chloride (MnCl2·4 H2O) and Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were from coriolis reagent. Sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2 H2O) was from Aa-laddin Reagent Co., Ltd.

CHI-660E (Shanghai Chenhua, China) was used to electrochemical detection. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, H7700, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, SU8220, Japan) were used to characterize the material morphology and analyze the element composition, respectively.

Graphene oxide nanosheets (GO) was synthesized by modified Hummers and Offeman methods [35]. Firstly, Firstly, 50 mL of concentrated H2SO4 was mixed with 2.0 g of hydrochloric acid-treated graphite in a four-necked flask placing in an ice bath, then 9.0 g of KMnO4 was slowly added to react for 1 h under 4 ℃. Secondly, the reacted mixture was transferred to a water bath at 35 ℃ and kept for 2 h. Then, a large amount of deionized water and 20 mL of 30% hydrogen peroxide were slowly added. The resulting product was filtered and washed with 5% dilute hydrochloric acid and deionized water until the solution was neutral, and dried at 80 ℃ for 24 h. Finally, GO was successfully prepared by ultrasonic dispersion.

rGO-β-CD was synthesized via a one-pot process as previously reported [36]. Briefly, GO (20 mg) was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and then β-CD (200 mg) was added in the above solution. After ultrasonic dispersion, 5 wt% Na2CO3 was added to adjust the pH to 9–10 and reacted for 24 h to obtained GO-β-CD. Finally, NaBH4 (16 mg) and MnCl2 (0.2 mM) were added and heated reflux for 1 h, and then 1 M HCl and H2O2 (30%) were added to remove metal ions. After reaction, the product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, washed by deionized water, and dried for 12 h at 60 ℃ in vacuum to obtain the final rGO-β-CD composite.

The synthesis of MoO3 was modified according to the reference [37]. Firstly, 0.70 g Na2MoO4 was added into 65 mL of deionized water to form a uniform solution, and then 5 mL of hydrochloric acid (8 M) was added into the above solution until the pH of the solution is less than 7.0. Next, the above solution was transferred to the high-pressure reactor at 160 ℃ for 8 h. After the reaction, the product was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min, washed by deionized water until the solution is neutral, and dried for 12 h at 60 ℃ in vacuum, the final MoO3 powder was then obtained.

Firstly, rGO-β-CD (2 mg mL−1) was dispersed in deionized water by sonication for 0.5 h. Then, 6 μL of GQDs solution and 6 μL of the rGO-β-CD dispersion were ultrasonic mixing uniformity to obtain rGO-β-CD/GQDs.

Firstly, the glass carbon electrode (GCE) is polished to make its surface smooth and clean. Next, 6 μL of rGO-β-CD/GQDs was dripped on GCE to prepare rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE modified electrodes. Then, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE was immerged in MoO3 dispersion, and 15 cycles of cyclic voltammetry at a voltage range of − 1.6–2.0 V, and finally the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE was prepared. For comparison, bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, rGO-β-CD/GCE, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE, MoO3/GCE were obtained through the same operation process.

The morphological features was characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, MoO3 possesses a uniform rod-like structures, which is consistent with the literature [37]. As shown in Fig. 1B, GO is a multilayer sheet structure with obvious folds, which indicated that GO has been successfully prepared. In Fig. 1C-D, we can find that rGO-β-CD shows an amorphous structure with staggered distribution of flakes and blocks, indicating that rGO and β-CD was successfully combined. Due to the addition of GQDs, it did not change the previous structure of rGO-β-CD with interlaced distribution of sheets and blocks. In addition, the addition of GQDs changed the surface structure of rGO-β-CD to have a rougher surface, and all the results indicated that GQDs were successfully introduced into the rGO-β-CD material (Fig. 1E-F). The rGO-β-CD/GQDs were successfully prepared.

XRD was used to reveal their crystal structures, the curves of rGO-β-CD, GO and MoO3 as shown in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1A, the spectrum of GO has a very strong and wide peak at 21.2° and small spike peak at 42.7°, which are corresponding to (002) and (100) of graphene layer, respectively [38,39]. In addition, it can be clearly observed that there is a sharp diffraction peak at 8.5°. Compared with untreated multilayer graphite, the relatively large d-spacing of GO may be affected by the entry of water molecules and the formation of oxygen-containing functional groups, indicating that GO has been successfully prepared. Conversely, rGO-β-CD, a sharp diffraction peak at 8.5° has disappeared, and the peak at 21.2° is increased, indicating that GO was successfully restored to rGO. The characteristic derivative peaks of MoO3 we prepared were consistent with standard α- MoO3 (JCPDS No. 05–0508) [40] (Fig. S1B).

As shown in Fig. S2A, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in GO and rGO-β-CD. The peak at 3367 cm−1 on the GO curve belongs to the tensile vibration absorption peak of O-H, and the characteristic peak of CO stretching vibration at 1723 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1 are related to the skeleton extension of C-OH groups. Multiple groups of peaks within the range of 1050–1230 cm−1 can be classified as the characteristic peaks of O-H deformation vibration, C-O vibration, C-O-C stretching vibration and other functional groups [41]. In addition, compared with GO, the intensity of most corresponding peaks in rGO-β-CD are reduced and some peak positions are shifted at 1000–1500 cm−1, and some small sub peaks appear, such as 1412 cm−1 (C-OH deformation vibration) and 1155 cm−1 (C-O-C stretching vibration), which indicated that some oxygen functional groups of GO have been reduced. In addition, the intensity of C-OH stretching vibration peak (1550 cm−1) is enhanced in rGO-β-CD, which may be due to the doped of β-CD. In Fig. S2B, there is a strong and wide peak at 3307 cm−1, which can be considered as the stretching vibration peak of hydroxyl group on β-CD ring. At the same time, we can observe its bending vibration at 1430 cm−1. The weak absorption at 2930 cm−1 can be considered as the C-H stretching vibration of the skeleton on the glucose unit, the small peak at 1650 cm−1 can be considered as the stretching vibration of CO, and the sharp peak at 1020 cm−1 can be considered as the stretching vibration of the glycoside bond in its molecule [42].

The elemental composition of the MoO3 was characterized by XPS. The curve of MoO3 (Fig. S3A) shows that consist of O and Mo elements. In the Mo 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. S3B), two peaks at 236.5, and 233.4 eV, which belong to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2, respectively [43]. The peak at 531.3 eV belongs to the binding energy of O1s (Fig. S3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) was used to characterize the interfacial properties and impedance changes between different modified electrodes and bare electrode. In [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution, the bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, rGO-β-CD/GCE, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE, MoO3/GCE, and rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE were obtained different Nyquist diagrams ( Fig. 2 A). Diameter of semicircle in high frequency region in EIS curve, which corresponded to charge transfer resistance (Rct). Therefore, as shown in Fig. 2 A, the Rct of the bare GCE is the largest which indicated that bare GCE with the worst conductivity. Due to the rGO with good conductivity, rGO-β-CD shown the semicircle with smaller diameter, which indicates that it has low Rct and strong electrochemical properties. Introduction of GQDs reduces the conductivity of the electrode (rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE). However, GQDs with excellent stability and compatibility, which improved the stability and uniformity of composites. Subsequently, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used for Que detection at GCE with different material modifications (Fig. 2B). The six different modified electrodes have different responses to Que. In Fig. 2B (a), a pair of very small redox peaks of Que is found on the bare GCE because of its weak adsorption on the electrode. Due to the poor electrical conductivity of GQDs, a pair of smaller redox peaks are found on GQDs/GCE (b). On the contrary, the redox peak of Que can be observed very clearly in both MoO3 electrocatalytic activity and rGO-β-CD with better conductivity (d, e). However, due to the poor electrochemical properties of GQDs, the redox current of Que in rGO-β-CD/GQDs/GCE decrease to a certain extent (c). Notably, because of the addition of GQDs, the composite rGO-β-CD/GQDs has better stability and dispersion properties. Compared with other modified electrodes, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE (−12.39 μA)(f) was shown excellent response to Que, because of rGO-β-CD with excellent conductivity, sheet structure of larger specific surface area and with cavity structure of β-CD with a certain adsorption enrichment effect, as well as electrocatalytic activity of MoO3 nanostructures which greatly improved the response to Que.

The modification amount of MoO3 on the electrode surface is controlled by the number of cycles of electrodeposition cycle, and its content is related to the excellent electrochemical performance of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/ MoO3/GCE. Hence, we studied the effect of different electrodeposition cycles on the detection ability of Que. As shown in Fig. S4C, the sensor has the best electrochemical performance to Que when the number of MoO3 electrodeposition cycles is 15. On the contrary, when too much MoO3 is accumulated on the electrode surface, it is not conducive to the electronic conduction of Que on the electrode surface. Therefore, we choose 15 cycles of electrodeposition as the best number of cycles.

The relationship between different acidity and peak potential were also studied in BPS with 5 μM Que on rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE. ( Fig. 3 A). when the pH increased from 4.5 to 6.0, the Ipa gradually increased. When pH= 6.0, the greatest Ipa was obtained. So, future studies are conducted at pH = 6.0. However, the Ipa gradually decreased when the pH increased to 8.0 (Fig. S4D). In addition, When pH increases, the Epa and Epc both moved negatively, which indicated that the involvement of protons in the electrochemical reaction. There is also a certain linear relationship between them (Epa, Epc, Eθ and pH), which can be expressed by the following linear equation (Fig. 3B).
Epa = − 0.0660 pH + 0.635
R
2
= 0.998
Epc = − 0.0631 pH + 0.559 (
R
2
= 0.996 )
E
θ
= − 0.0645 pH + 0.597 (
R
2
= 0.999 )

The above results shows that the EƟ-pH slope of Que is − 0.0645 V/pH, which is close to the theoretical value of − 0.0591 V/pH, indicated that the process of Que detection is an isoproton-isoelectron processes. The mechanism of Que in rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE is shown in Scheme S1.( Scheme 1).

In order to explore whether enrichment has an impact on the detection of Que content, the accumulation time and potential were tested separately using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV, 2.5 μM Que). Firstly, the peak current value increases with the increase of enrichment time, the oxidation peak current significant increases from 0 s to 450 s (Fig. S4B), and reaches the maximum peak current value at 450 s. However, when the enrichment time exceeds 450 s, the oxidation peak current decreases slightly. This is because the absorption of Que by composite reaches the maximum and saturation occurs. Therefore, the optimal enrichment time is 450 s. In addition, when the potential (Fig. S4A) increased from − 0.3 V to − 0.1 V, the adsorption-enrichment capacity of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE on Que gradually increased, leading to continuous increase in the oxidation peak current (Ipa). However, the Ipa gradually decreased when the potential increased again. So, the − 0.1 V has been chosen as optimal potential. From the above research, the subsequent experimental studies were chosen at 450 s and − 0.1 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE at different scan rates
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the redox mechanism of Que on rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE, tests were performed at different scan rates (v) in 0.1 M PBS (5 μM Que). As v rises from 0.02 to 0.40 V s-1, the peak current rises steadily ( Fig. 4 A) and is linearly related to v (Fig. 4B). The linear relationship are as follows.
Ipc
μ A
= 0.0588 V + 0.318
( R
2
= 0.996 )
Ipa
μ A
= − 0.0519 V + 0.339 (
R
2
= 0.998 )

The above results show that the electrochemical reaction process of Que at rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE is adsorption-controlled process. The potential changes with increasing v, demonstrating that the electron transfer of Que on rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE is an irreversible redox process. The linear relationship is as follows.
Epa (v) = 0·0108 lnv (mV/s) + 0·2112 (R2=0·997)

According to the Laviron equation [44]:
Epa
v
=
E
θ
+
RT
α nF
+ ln
RT
k
θ
α nF
+
RT
α nF
lnv

The slope value is about 0.0108, n is about 2 (Fig. 4 C), which indicated that 2 electrons were transferred during the whole electrochemical reaction process. This result is consistent with the literature [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative Analysis of Que on rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
The DPV technique for different concentrations of Que on rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE sensor was investigated under optimal experimental conditions ( Fig. 5 A). As Que increases from 5 to 2600 nM, the Ipa increases and the LOD is 1.5 nM (S/N = 3). As shown in Fig. 5B, the concentration of Que was linearly related to the Ipa in the range of 0.005–2.60 μM. The linear relationship is as follows.
Ipa (μA) = - 0·0106 C (μM) + 0·3653 (0·005 μM ≤ C ≤ 2·60 μM R2 = 0·993)

The results indicated that rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE has a better detection accuracy than other Que sensors ( Table 1). In addition, the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE electrochemical sensor can be used to detect Que with wider detection range and lower detection line.

In order to determine the performance of the sensor we built, good repeatability, reproducibility and excellent selectivity are one of the important evaluation indexes. Multiple parallel experiments with relative standard deviation (RSD=2.2%) and DPV data of seven different modified electrodes (rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE) with RSD is 4.3%, which indicated that the sensor has good repeatability and good reproducibility ( Fig. 6A-B). In addition, the anti-interference ability of Que was also studied by DPV technique. 50 times of ions (Mg2+, SO4 2-, Zn2+, NO3 -), 25 times of glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), gallic acid (Ga), L-cysteine (L-cys), sucrose (Suc), citric acid (CA) and oxalic acid (OA) were added into the PBS containing 1 μM Que, the Ipa was little changed to detect Que. At the same, 1 μM hesperidin (HP) and diosmetin (DS) were added, similar results were obtained with the signal changes were all within 10%. All the results indicated that the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE has great selectivity for of Que (Fig. 6 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Que in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The drinks (Huiyuan orange juice (①) and Sea buckthorn (②)), were from supermarket next to xiangtan university. Firstly, the drinks were been diluted with PBS solution. Subsequently, Que of different concentrations were added to the solution. DPV technology was used to record the experimental results (Table S1).

Natural sample (pomegranate (③)) was also been detected. Firstly, peel and juicer processing and filtered. Next, 10 μL filtrate was added into PBS solution. Subsequently, Que of different concentrations were added to the solution. DPV technology was used to record the experimental results (Table S1). The recoveries rate of the Que is 94∼105%. All the results indicated that the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE can be used for the detection of Que in actual samples.

The rGO-β-CD was prepared through a simple one pot strategy and then used to construct an ultrasensitive rGO-β-CD sensor. Due to the outstanding conductivity, sheet structure of rGO-CD, and the cavity structure of β-CD and MoO3 with good electrocatalytic activity, the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE exhibited better conductivity and electroactivity. In addition, the rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3/GCE exhibited the excellent peak current and satisfactoried performance of Que with wide linear range (5–2600 nM) and lower LOD (1.5 nM). Besides, rGO-β-CD/GQDs/MoO3 showed stability and sensitive detection in the detection of real sample. These excellent properties demonstrate that our prepared rGO-β-CD and MoO3 have more possibilities in food and medicine applications.

Li Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoling Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Jiejun Li: Investigation, Data curation. Yuhong Li: Resources, Investigation, Validation. Jia Chen: Resources, Investigation. Mengting Zhang: Resources, Investigation. Jaqi Shi: Formal analysis. Pingping Yang: Resources, Investigation. Pengcheng Zhao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yixi Xie: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Junjie Fei: Supervision, Formal analysis, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 22176160, 21874114, and 21475114), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ30662), the Project of Innovation Team of the Ministry of Education (IRT_17R90), the Project of Science and Technology Innovation Plan of Hunan Province (2021WK2006), and the Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province (CX20210632).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.130757.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
